review: just going to come out and say it i love the game and i have it on multiple platforms i don't usually play it on steam but over all iv played since release and a lot has change but a lot has also stayed the same though some may think the game is a dumpster fire its truly a gem don't get me wrong iv reached apex predator twice and once as lifleline the other time as octain. iv played all of the legends and iv played it on every platform it is available on. the gameplay is amazing. the mechanics although it takes time to learn is impressive and a huge positive. don't get me wrong i understand plenty of toxic players play the game but unfortunately every game has them. matchmaking could still use improvement but its a skill based game so at the end of the day you get better by facing harder opponents not easy ones so the only thing you can do is keep playing and get good or give up on beating those sweaty players and just don't engage unless you have to. i have seen the play style, the meta, the maps, and everything change over time but one thing never changes recognizing that it is a team game and that if you don't communicate its almost impossible to win. if you don't like teamwork stick to solos or duos. i do hope they will add more to the game and possibly go back to the roots for a couple of things but overall i highly recommend the game with the simple notion in mind that you will probably rage quit.

review:
I’ve been playing this game for a couple of years and never had any issues. I recently upgraded my PC with new components because I was excited to get better performance and enjoy the game even more. Instead, the moment I launched the game after upgrading my hardware, I was banned for “cheating.”

I have never cheated. I only installed new PC parts, and somehow that was enough for the system to flag my account. What makes this even worse is that there is no real appeal process, so loyal players can lose their accounts without any fair review.

It’s extremely disappointing to spend years supporting a game, only to be punished for upgrading your computer. A system that treats hardware changes like cheating, without properly investigating, is unfair and frustrating.

Until they improve their anti-cheat and give players a real chance to appeal false bans, I can’t recommend this game.

review: Apex Legends is truly a masterpiece… if your goal is to experience emotional damage in digital form. From the moment you load in, the game kindly places you against players who seem to have trained with John Wick himself, while your teammates are busy discovering what buttons do. The matchmaking system feels less like skill-based pairing and more like a social experiment to see how fast a person can lose sanity. Every gunfight is either over in 0.2 seconds or turns into a chaotic mess where you’re somehow getting shot from five directions at once. And don’t even worry about improving—because just when you think you’re getting better, the game gently reminds you that you are, in fact, not. The updates bravely introduce new bugs like it’s a feature, and the servers occasionally take a nap mid-match for extra suspense. Overall, Apex Legends delivers a truly unique experience: it’s not just a battle royale, it’s a test of patience, luck, and how much suffering you’re willing to call “fun.”

review:
大逃杀的游戏有非常的多，就像树上可以长苹果，可以长橘子，甚至可以长菠萝。但是总会有你最喜欢的那一种。当然，APEX 肯定不是我最喜欢的那一种。从游戏环境、玩家、玩法甚至是皮肤上，它总有太多让我挑剔的点。但就是这样的在意，才会让我去挑剔它。总体来说，如果满分是 5 分的话，3.5 分到 4 分是有的。
There are so many battle royale games, just like a tree can grow apples, oranges, even pineapples. But there’s always one you like the most. Of course, APEX is definitely not my favorite. It has too many things I’m picky about—from the game environment, players, gameplay, even the skins. But it’s exactly because I care that I’m critical of it. Overall, if the full score is 5, I’d give it somewhere between 3.5 and 4.

review:
I wake up each day, I'm thinking about when will I get my very first heirloom. I'm at work/running errands, I'm thinking about heirloom shards. I get home and log in to play apex and I'm thinking how many more apex packs do I need to finally get my hands on one.

I log out to go to sleep, I'm thinking about saving up packs. I finally go to sleep, I'm dreaming about seeing that red light from the packs.

I wake up.... and the entire cycle starts over. And again, and again and again.

But I do love Apex Legends.

It’s the only game where:
You hear one gunshot and suddenly 5 squads materialize
You win one fight and immediately get audited by the entire lobby
You finally get good loot and instantly die

Me after winning a fight:
“WE’RE BUILT DIFFERENT”

5 seconds later:
gets third-partied, fourth-partied, spiritually-partied

10/10 survival game.
Survival not guaranteed.

review:
Apple Fritters
Ingredients

For the fritters:

2 cups all-purpose flour

1/4 cup granulated sugar

2 tsp baking powder

1/2 tsp salt

1 tsp cinnamon

2/3 cup milk

2 large eggs

1 tsp vanilla extract

2 tbsp melted butter

2 cups apples (peeled, diced small — Granny Smith or Honeycrisp work great)

Oil for frying (vegetable or canola)

For the glaze:

1 1/2 cups powdered sugar

2–3 tbsp milk

1/2 tsp vanilla extract

Instructions

Make the batter

In a bowl, whisk together flour, sugar, baking powder, salt, and cinnamon.

In another bowl, mix milk, eggs, vanilla, and melted butter.

Combine wet and dry ingredients until just mixed (don’t overmix).

Fold in diced apples.

Heat the oil

Heat about 2–3 inches of oil in a deep pan to 350°F (175°C).

Fry the fritters

Drop batter by spoonfuls (about 1/4 cup each) into hot oil.

Fry 2–3 minutes per side until golden brown.

Remove and drain on paper towels.

Make the glaze

Mix powdered sugar, milk, and vanilla until smooth.

Glaze

While fritters are still warm, drizzle or dip in glaze

review:
played this game for way longer than i should’ve. across multiple platforms even. genuinely impressive how much time i managed to waste here. 

yeah, the game is good movement is smooth, shooting feels great, and with friends it can be really fun. that’s probably the problem, it tricks you into sticking around.

but man… everything around that is rough.

the skin system is straight up a scammy slot machine. you open packs and get flooded with useless junk no one wants, just so you keep buying more. “maybe the next one” type addiction.

optimization? awful.

and the playerbase now is just… exhausting.

at the end of the day, it’s way more grindy than it needs to be. i dumped too much time and money into this and honestly regret it. this game has become the embodiment of everything that went wrong with respawn.

good game buried under bad decisions. wouldn’t recommend.

review:
This is a fantastic BR with the worst monetization. 

Gunplay, movement, and Legend variety are all top tier. There’s a ton of fun combinations of gameplay styles that’s incredibly satisfying when you find the right synergy with your squad. There’s no shooter on the market that feels this good.

That said, monetization is… aggressive. Overpriced skins, bundles, Apex packs, event passes and split battle passes per season almost takes the fun out of playing. 

If BR isn’t your thing, there’s a handful of other modes that provide a good variety of ways to play the game.

7 years strong and still going. Highly recommend this, even if you’ve not jumped in yet.

review:
There’s a special kind of irony in watching Apex Legends stumble into a mech-themed collaboration while the game it spun off from — the beloved Titanfall series — lies abandoned. For longtime fans of Respawn’s work, Apex doesn’t just feel like another battle royale. It feels like the corporate gravestone sitting on top of Titanfall 3.

Let’s rewind. The Titanfall games had one of the most fluid FPS movement systems ever made: wall-running, double-jumping, and the pure adrenaline rush of calling down a massive Titan in the middle of combat. It was fast, inventive, and genuinely different from the endless parade of military shooters. The obvious next step was Titanfall 3. Fans waited. Rumors circulated. Hope lingered.

And then we got Apex Legends.

Instead of expanding on what made Titanfall special, Respawn pivoted into the already overcrowded battle royale genre. The movement system was stripped down. The Titans — the literal defining feature of the franchise — disappeared. What remained was a hero shooter glued onto a battle royale framework, designed more around seasonal battle passes and cosmetic storefronts than the kind of mechanical innovation that made Titanfall stand out.

But the real insult comes years later.

Now Apex is running a Gundam collaboration. Yes — giant robots. Mechs. The exact thing the Titanfall series was built around. Except instead of actually bringing Titans back or making the sequel fans have been begging for, they’re giving regular Apex characters mech-themed skins like they’re cosplaying the very concept the studio abandoned.

It’s almost impressive in its tone-deafness.

Imagine shelving the franchise famous for pilotable mechs, ignoring requests for a sequel for nearly a decade, and then proudly announcing mech cosmetics for a completely different game. It’s like putting racing stripes on a bicycle and calling it a Formula 1 tribute.

And that’s the broader problem with Apex: it feels less like a passion project and more like a live-service machine that swallowed a beloved franchise whole. Every season brings more limited-time skins, more monetization hooks, and more crossover events designed to keep players grinding rather than innovating the core gameplay.

Meanwhile, Titanfall 2 — arguably one of the best shooters of the 2010s — sits in the corner like a forgotten masterpiece. Its campaign is still praised, its movement still unmatched, and its multiplayer still remembered fondly by the people who stuck with it.

The tragedy isn’t that Apex exists. It’s that Apex exists instead of Titanfall 3.

And now, watching a Gundam crossover parade mech aesthetics around in a game that literally removed the franchise’s defining feature feels like the final punchline.

Apex didn’t just spin off from Titanfall.

It replaced it.

review: I have roughly 3k hours total in this game. many spent on console, and the swap to pc was not to harsh on my game play enjoyment. I have been a season one player on and off. Cheaters and hackers have been ruining ranked experiences for many people and even pro events. More time is spent in "content" which is over priced, over time more ruined, events. Played a ranked game this season, game crashed, and rejoined, to then be denied rejoining my game let alone I never got the option to rejoin, and was punished for something out of my control. Arenas will never return permanently, Duos ranked, solos and squads will never come, and we still never got our Loba swimsuit skin. EA doesn't consider their player base with updates, they consider the money they need to make more of compared to previous years. And to address aim assist, its not truly OP, but it needs some sort of different approach. YES pc players can move, but a controller player should have that much assist with very minimal effort.

review:
Tl;dr Apex Legends best Battle Royale game in my opinion. Fast gameplay, special characters, nearly good optimization makes this game better than other Battle Royale games. Even though it belongs to EA, it is free and there is no pay to win, which makes it very special.

Apex Legend's gameplay faster than other fps games and this feature make me feel good. The movement system is versatile, knowing or not knowing makes such a difference that you will be surprised. Their weapons are diverse and there is a high probability that you will not find a weapon that suits your style.

The visual style looks very good, it neither tires the eyes nor looks too simple. Their voices are well designed, and even the walking sound of each character is different, so you can tell who is near you just by walking.

My computer crashes when I play these days, it's a problem I've never experienced before, other than that, I don't have any optimization problems in the game, they have done a very good job.

Overall Apex Legends is best Battle Royale game right now and you could play. This game's style, gameplay, sound design are peak off its genre

review: Apex Legends is a battle royale that is like no other battle royale out there. A team-based game that has a skill ceiling that no one can replicate. It is not friendly to new users because the people who play everyday hit the skill ceiling for playing years of apex. This game though out the years always changes the game every season update. Season 0 was very different from where it is today. Sometimes I do wish we had a solo mode because that can really test your might and see if you can make it to the top. Sometimes you can get unlucky by a third party kill, or when you fall into the map and everyone else has a weapon besides you. and you can just hop around and run because the game doesn't punish you for moving a lot. This game you can do wall jumps, climb any wall with any legend. Slide down hill and do a little bit of a bunny hop. You can just dance around your enemies as octane and will the fight because you are so fast and the bullets miss. Sometimes you get toxic teammates who don't help you out because they wanna kill the team and you can't so anything about it in rank but in pubs just leave go to another because you don't have to deal with these people. You ask for heals sometimes no one gives you heals and sometimes you have to scavenge for some. This game is recommend to play all the time because how fast pace action you can get. games can end in 30 seconds or less. I can't stand the teammates that leave as soon as you go down. you know we are playing a team game that requires us to win? But any who love this game to death. 10/10 I need teammates, good ones who work together. <3
label: Positive

review:
When this game first came out, it was in my opinion the most enjoyable take on the Battle Royale format I had seen. They originated a respawn mechanic that I hadn't seen the competition think about, allowing your team to have a comeback if you could manage to grab their banner and bring it to a respawn point, and some of those moments back then will live in my memory for a long time.
In recent years, though, Respawn- NOT EA, but RESPAWN, has taken the soul out of this game, and turned it into a money dispenser. Collab after collab, overpriced battlepasses and Heirloom skins you can only surely get by spending $300+ on each event, or gamble all your money away to MAYBE get something you want. And unrelated to this, but I think as many as three prototypes for what COULD HAVE become Titanfall 3 have been axed just through this game's lifetime (can't hold that against Apex, I'm just salty that Titanfall is dead).

If you decide to give this game a try, please do everyone a favor and don't spend a single penny on the trash they try to shovel at you, don't give them more money to ruin this IP with.

extremely helpful in a gun game. my teammate by the way dropped somewhere in a completely different postal code got knocked instantly and is now spam pinging like a dumb ******. ping ping ping ping ping. my brother you are already gone. meanwhile the enemy somehow has an r-99 lvl 300 badge a wingman purple shields 400 damage and movement so aggressive it feels like he personally logged on today to ruin my emotional stability. it’s honestly a really fun game though as long as you are not fighting a bald ttv wraith who has been living inside a zipline building since release 2020 and moves like a caffeinated ******. i would only recommend apex legends to people who feel like they currently have a little too much happiness in their life. i originally opened the game for a few relaxed rounds very casual very normal gaming experience but now i’m still here because apparently my brain chemistry is built around suffering. apex is also that game where you say “ok one more round” and then suddenly three hours have passed and you’re sitting there in complete silence wondering if your aim is actually bad or if your mouse has developed authism. i also always land exactly where 17 other teams land not because it’s smart but because every single time my brain goes “this time you win the 50/50.” i do not win the 50/50. my special ability is also not movement or game sense my special ability is finding exactly the one weapon i cannot use. wingman i am hitting air molecules. r-99 i am drawing beautiful artistic outlines around enemies. snipers i am basically just politely warning them that violence might occur soon. the movement in apex is still beautiful though walljumps slides tap strafes neo strafe bunny jumps but i personally get stuck in doorframes like a roomba. my main role in the squad is moral support while my teammates clutch a heroic 1v3 i am lying knocked somewhere 40 meters away aggressively pinging and providing emotional encouragement. also every enemy that kills me is always “one shot” which apparently means he still has shields health a loving family and long term career plans. but sometimes everything clicks sometimes you actually hit your shots sometimes you wipe a squad and for a brief moment you feel like the main character in an apex trailer and that exact moment is when the game grabs your soul. also small side note this game makes my pc sound like 911 and the moment an octane runs at me i already know it’s over because that man is moving at mach 3 half on crack sliding around corners and delivering a combo that makes me reconsider my will to exist but overall apex legends is a fantastic game for people who are masochists.

review:
Your money is better spent on Titanfall 2 than the microtransactions in this game. You get so much more for 30$ compared to the 500$ trying to get an heirloom. Or even 30$ worth of loot ticks. 

It can't even run on the steam deck anymore. Titanfall 2 can. 

I invested so much time when I was in high school because I wanted to try and become a pro, and now it feels wasted. 
Don't get me wrong the skill carried over to other games, but after I looked at the bigger picture, played Titanfall 2 and saw what could have been I was absolutely disgusted. 

Apex was my first shooter game (Unless you count pixel gun 3d) so the bar was set pretty low. And as I expanded and played other shooters it became more and more apparent how awful apex is as a game. Top that with the abrasive marketing and it honestly just feels like a ♥♥♥♥♥♥ mobile game with ads. They have done several colabs with IPs that don't even fit the Apex universe and completely ruins the immersion and completely tarnished the Titanfall lore. 

The only thing I can actually get behind is the LGBTQIA+ representation. That's it. Everything else in this game is hot garbage. The high skill floor movement with incredibly low skill ceiling movement, every characters kit does not feel fun and pales in comparison to Titanfall 2's kits and kit customization. 

Let me be clear. Respawn says they love Titanfall 2 but then always deletes comments talking about it or praising it. 

They do not love it. And they want to burry it. All the good people who worked on Titanfall left the studio and any semblance of Titanfall is gone. As long as apex exists, Titanfall will always be ignored. 

Turned into a rant but, if you truly share my view then I beg you. Delete your account, back charge your transactions, do as I did today and leave a negative review, and most importantly.. don't look back.

review:
I have been playing fairly consistently since s2 (on ps, migrated to pc. I have 1600 hours on ps) and i can say this game is in an alright place right now. I still have a lot of fun and can rank up easily. Guns feel good. There aren't things to me that feel incredibly unfair or unbalanced, but it was definitely more fun in the past. A lot of (unintentionally added) movement tech has been removed in favor of the quicktime vault, which is sad but also lowers the skill floor which is good for others. Movement feels a bit clunkier than it used to, I don't entirely understand why. 
But, what I miss most of all is the lore. It's just collabs and skins, now-- nothing story wise. They fired a lot of their writers and a lot of staff. They fired the person who wrote Sparrow's story the day after his season launch. It's missing that extra care it used to have. No comics, no script, no nothing.
And, If you are a new player, you will experience a new level of fresh hell as you try to catch up to unemployed sweats and whales that own every wraith and octane skin. If you have a squad to play with you will probably like the fast gunplay. Wildcard is a lot of fun, good way to practice since engagements are always happening. Otherwise, you might find it to be agonizing. 
SO! In short I don't recommend this game to new players, or maybe even returning players who haven't played since season 9 or something. It's changed a lot and can be hard to pick back up. But it has a special place in my heart and I still love it.

review:
Perfect Fish Paella Recipe in 10 Easy Steps:
1. Heat 2 tbsp olive oil in a wide pan.
2. Cook 1 chopped onion for about 5 minutes until soft.
3. Add 3 minced garlic cloves and 1 diced red pepper; cook for 2–3 minutes.
4. Stir in grated tomatoes and 1 tsp smoked paprika; cook until thick.
5. Add 300 g paella rice (e.g., Bomba rice) and toast for 1 minute.
6. Pour in 750 ml fish stock with saffron and spread the rice evenly.
7. Do not stir after this point; simmer for about 10 minutes.
8. Place fish chunks, prawns, mussels, and squid on top.
9. Cook for 8–10 minutes until the seafood is done and the mussels open.
10. Increase the heat briefly to create a crispy base (socarrat), rest for 5 minutes, then serve with lemon and parsley.

review:
Despite all the screams of "dead game", this game has reached the same status as Fortnite. It has lived out the maximum for its core gameplay and now exists off of collabs and side gamemodes. This all sounds pretty bad, but the core game is still great. Balancing is never settled, there's always some sort of tweak that throws the entire game into a new dynamic each season. Legends are reworked and re-tooled all the time.

The guns feel satisfying to shoot. Each one fills a niche and, if you're not satisfied, rip another off of the corpse of an enemy. Weapon droughts have been partially solved by the way of weapon racks that also offer weapons based off of ammo type and a free upgrade at the side of the kiosk. It is clear that the devs are doing a dangerous balancing act each time they add a new mechanic and I, for one, am glad that they keep trying.

There is a massive emphasis on movement, the game is purely positioning first and how fast you can rotate to advantageous ground is more important than how good you are with a gun. The devs have made super-gliding more accessible, but mastering the abilities of each legend and planning how to move your team is paramount.

Whether you believe this game killed Titanfall or not, it is still, in my opinion, a fun game. Would it be better if it was divorced from Titanfall? Maybe. It is heartbreaking to see the R6-ification of a once expansive sci-fi military universe. Legends (the playable characters of this game) aren't soldiers, they're money hungry, attention hogging, photo-op obsessed sportsmen. They are playing a glorified game of airsoft.

review:
7/10 - Fun gameplay... the rest of the package not so much.

I've played Apex Legends for three periods in my life. First when it was released, then sometime in the middle of season 13 or around that and finally recently, in season 27-28. I can say that over the time this game just feels overly streamlined, way too much. Playing the game in bursts instead of continuously over the seasons did help me feel see across the live-service, instead of being in the midst like other ones I've played like Rainbow Six, or even League.

But first, let's talk about the good. The gameplay is good, 90% due to the incredible gunplay, which alone is what gave me the urge to come back time and time again. There's nothing better than 3-tapping someone with a Wingman and that has stayed true from the beginning to now. Movement and the abilities are another bright spot.

Now, for the rest... the live-service model. This is a double edge sword, because I don't see this game existing as something other than a live-service, but wow, how terrible it feels. Every time I came back to this game the game felt more and more souless, with currencies, menus, and events designed for maximum profit above fun or all else. 

This last burst I've played, I couldn't help to feel that I was logging in to a mobile game every time I went into the main menu. I know this is how all the live services are moving to, but again, the big intervals between playing really made it more blatant to me. We didn't have 4 different currencies, 3 tiers of battle pass and some kind of event gatcha mechanic at season 0. I will never condemn a company just because they are introducing means to make a profit on their product, but there is a clear line to trying to make a profit and squeezing your audience, and Apex does cross it.

I'm lucky that I never really vibed with the characters that much (nothing against them, but except for a few 3 or 4, they aren't really appealing to me), so when I logged in in season 13 or in the season 27, I just got overwhelmed by the amount of things trying to be sold to me and never really spent money on this game.

But again, this is all part of the live-service pipeline that I so much hate. I know that it's cool that we can have free games like this for everyone to play, but I definitely don't think that these games are free at all, if you are remotely interested in them. Either by spending a couple of bucks in a skin here and there, then getting the passes, which are a "great value", or spending too much time in the overly streamlined design that makes you somewhat addicted to the game, the sunken cost fallacy will get you and this game will soon become a task and not a fun game. I don't have an issue if this is what you want doing, I know that there are people who just play one game and that's it, but what I'm saying it's that this is not really for me, as I've been a part of this pipeline for multiple games and it really felt freeing leaving them behind.

So, why the hell, in plain 2026, am I writing an honest review for an established 7 year-old game that is part of an established model that is clearly not for me? Well, I don't really know, I just like writing reviews and maybe (most definitely) I'm just stupid and silly. What I'm trying to say in this whole blabber of text is that the game is fun, and if you can look past or even enjoy the provided live-service model, you will have a great time. Hell, I can't stand the model and here I am enjoying myself, so that must mean something, right?

review:
Apex Legends, developed by Respawn Entertainment and published by Electronic Arts, is one of the most polished and fast-paced battle royale games available today. Since its release in 2019, it has managed to stand out in a crowded genre thanks to its unique combination of hero-based abilities, smooth movement mechanics, and strong team gameplay.

One of the biggest strengths of the game is its Legend system. Each character has their own abilities, which adds strategy to every match. For example, Bloodhound can scan for enemies, while Wraith can create portals and escape dangerous situations. This variety encourages players to experiment with different playstyles and team combinations.

Another highlight is the movement system, which feels incredibly smooth. Sliding, climbing, zip-lining, and fast gunplay make fights feel intense and dynamic. Compared to many other battle royale games, Apex Legends rewards skillful positioning and quick reactions.

review:
The Gold Standard of Movement Shooters! 🔥🧤

Apex Legends isn't just another Battle Royale; it's a high-speed, ability-based masterpiece. If you're tired of slow-paced shooters, the movement mechanics here—like sliding and wall-jumping—will keep you hooked.

Ratings:

Movement: 10/10 (Smooth and satisfying)

Gunplay: 9/10 (Recoil patterns are rewarding to learn)

Teamwork: 10/10 (The Ping system is still the best in the industry)

Visuals: 8/10 (Unique art style that stays clean during fights)

Pros:

Unique Legends: Each character has a distinct personality and kit that can change the outcome of a fight.

Ping System: You can communicate perfectly with teammates without even using a microphone.

Fast-paced Action: The "Third-party" risk makes every fight intense and high-stakes.

Free-to-Play: One of the most polished free games on Steam.

Cons:

High Skill Ceiling: You will get destroyed by "Predators" when you first start.

Server Issues: Occasional lag or disconnects during peak hours.

Final Verdict: The best movement mechanics in any Battle Royale. Whether you're playing casual or ranked, the adrenaline rush is real. 9.5/10!

review:
Apex Legends is a free-to-play battle royale shooter developed by Respawn Entertainment and published by Electronic Arts. Released in 2019, the game quickly became one of the most popular titles in the battle royale genre thanks to its fast gameplay and strong focus on teamwork.

Gameplay

One of the biggest strengths of Apex Legends is its smooth and fast-paced gameplay. Players compete in squads of three and choose from different “Legends,” each with unique abilities. These abilities add a strategic layer to the game, meaning that teamwork and smart use of skills are just as important as aiming.

The movement system is also excellent. Sliding, climbing, and moving quickly across the map make the game feel dynamic and exciting. In addition, the game features an innovative ping system that allows players to communicate with teammates without using voice chat.

Graphics and Design

The game has a stylized but detailed visual style. The maps are large and varied, with many vertical areas and interesting locations. Weapon animations and sound design are polished, which helps create an immersive experience.

Strengths

Fast and smooth gameplay

Unique characters with special abilities

Excellent ping communication system

Free to play

Weaknesses

Can be difficult for new players at first

Some seasons feel repetitive

Microtransactions can be expensive

Conclusion

Overall, Apex Legends stands out as one of the best battle royale games available today. Its combination of fast movement, unique characters, and strong team mechanics makes it exciting and competitive. Although it can be challenging for beginners, it offers a very rewarding experience once players get used to the mechanics.

⭐ Rating: 9/10

deterrent.

cheat transparency and management response, the game's longevity is at risk.

review:
You know when an MMO is near its death, the dev/publisher starts milking the game. They focus too much on microtransactions while ignoring crucial problems. Leading to the player base shrinking and the game dying. Yeah, that’s what’s happening to Apex Legends.

Once that happens, they’ll eventually pull the plug on the servers, effectively killing the game. And with the absence of an official end-of-life plan, Apex will just be lost (I know about the modded version, but it's unofficial).

I won’t even get into the server problems, game balance, battle-pass change, the dumb ranking system, the game-breaking bugs, cheating, or the cheater who hacked his way into an ALGS pro match. You can check Reddit for all of that.

They just keep milking the game, and the spare money gets spent on a Final Fantasy collab and some very, very NSFW Loba skins. Apparently, that’s priority number one.

The game is fun at times. It has nice movement, which is no shocker since it’s built on a modified version of the Source Engine, which itself is based on the Quake engine. But, once your friends slowly go offline, you'll find little to no reason to comback.

review:
[h1] Apex Legends – A Veteran Player’s Steam Review After Too Many Drops [/h1]

The first time I played Apex Legends, I thought it was just another battle royale.

Then I accidentally slid down a hill, hip-fired an R-99, and watched my teammate ping a shield battery for me while yelling “third party incoming.”

That was the moment I realized Apex isn’t just about shooting.

It’s about movement, positioning, and those chaotic moments where three squads collide and nobody really knows how the fight is going to end.

Years later, after hundreds of matches, certain things still immediately trigger memories for veteran players.

Like the sound of a shield crack.

Or hearing a Pathfinder grapple above you.

Or seeing a Wraith portal appear in the middle of a fight and realizing something terrible is about to happen.

[hr][/hr]

[h2] The Movement Is Still the Best in Any Battle Royale [/h2]

Every veteran Apex player eventually realizes the same thing.

[b]Gun skill matters, but movement wins fights.[/b]

Sliding off rooftops.
Bunny hopping into cover.
Climbing walls to escape a push.

One of the most satisfying mechanics in the game is chaining movement together during a fight.

Sliding downhill while reloading.
Jumping around a corner to avoid damage.
Climbing a ledge to break line of sight.

You start to realize the map isn’t just terrain.

It’s a playground.

[i]A lot of experienced players say Apex feels less like a shooter and more like a movement game that happens to include guns.[/i]

[hr][/hr]

[h2] The Sound of a Third Party [/h2]

Veteran players know this moment instantly.

You just finished wiping a squad.

Your armor is cracked.
Your teammate is reviving someone.
You’re trying to loot quickly.

Then you hear distant gunfire getting closer.

Footsteps.

A zipline.

And someone says the sentence every Apex player has heard a thousand times:

[b]“Third party.”[/b]

Suddenly the entire situation changes.

That quiet moment after a fight turns into pure chaos again.

Apex might be the only game where winning a fight actually makes you nervous.

Because it almost guarantees another squad is already on the way.

[hr][/hr]

[h2] The Weapons Everyone Has a Love-Hate Relationship With [/h2]

Ask veteran players about certain weapons and you’ll get instant reactions.

The R-99 shredding someone before they even react.
The Wingman headshot that completely flips a fight.
The Peacekeeper hitting a massive pump for 99 damage.

There’s also that one moment everyone remembers.

Missing an entire magazine at close range.

Then getting knocked immediately afterward.

[strike]We don’t talk about those moments.[/strike]

But they happen.

And somehow they still make the game fun.

[hr][/hr]

[h2] The Legends That Change Everything [/h2]

Every experienced Apex player has a story about certain Legend abilities changing a fight completely.

A Wraith portal saving the entire squad.
A Gibraltar dome turning an impossible fight into a winnable one.
A Pathfinder grapple launching someone into a perfect flank.

Those abilities create moments that simply wouldn’t happen in most shooters.

[u]A good ability at the right moment can completely shift the outcome of a fight.[/u]

That’s why Apex fights often feel unpredictable even when you think you’re winning.

[hr][/hr]

[h2] The “One HP and a Dream” Moment [/h2]

Every veteran Apex player knows this situation.

You’re the last player alive on your team.

Both teammates are down.

Your armor is broken.
You have almost no health.

But somehow you manage to knock one enemy.

Then another.

Then suddenly the fight is over.

Your teammates are yelling in voice chat.

And you're just sitting there staring at the screen thinking:

[b]“How did that actually work?”[/b]

Those moments are what people remember long after the match ends.

[hr][/hr]

[h2] The Small Details Only Long-Time Players Notice [/h2]

There are little things in Apex that veteran players instantly recognize.

The sound of armor swapping during a fight.

Someone popping a Phoenix Kit behind cover.

A Horizon lift suddenly appearing in the middle of a battle.

Or the terrifying moment when you hear a Revenant totem placed nearby.

Those details add layers to the fights that casual players might not notice immediately.

But experienced players react to them instantly.

[i]Sometimes you hear something before you even see the enemy.[/i]

[hr][/hr]

[h2] Why People Still Play After Thousands of Matches [/h2]

A lot of battle royale games lose their excitement over time.

But Apex manages to keep players engaged because the fights are never identical.

Every match produces different stories.

A clutch revive.
A chaotic third party.
A squad wipe that shouldn’t have been possible.

Those moments are unpredictable.

And that unpredictability is what keeps players coming back.

[hr][/hr]

[h2] Final Thoughts [/h2]

Apex Legends is one of the rare multiplayer games where the gameplay still feels exciting even after hundreds of matches.

The movement system is incredible.
The team mechanics are smart.
And the fights constantly create moments that players remember.

It isn’t perfect.

But when everything comes together, Apex delivers some of the most intense multiplayer moments in modern shooters.

[b]Personal Rating: 9 / 10[/b]

A battle royale where movement, teamwork, and chaotic fights combine to create moments that veteran players never forget.

review: From the moment you drop in, you realize this game is built around coordination. Every Legend has unique abilities, and winning isn’t about running around solo  it’s about combining abilities, making smart rotations, calling out enemy positions, and clutching fights together. A perfectly timed ult combo or a clean revive under pressure feels insane. And let’s talk about difficulty this game is HARD. Movement is fast and unforgiving. Gunfights are intense. Positioning matters. One bad decision and you’re back in the lobby. But that’s what makes it addictive. When your squad locks in and you wipe a team with perfect communication? Unreal. Ranked mode takes it to another level. The higher you climb, the smarter and faster players get. It’s not casual run-and-gun  it’s strategy, awareness, and pure mechanical skill. If you want a game that challenges you and pushes you to actually improve, Apex delivers. The ping system alone makes teamwork smooth, even without a mic. It’s one of the best communication systems in any shooter. Add in tight gunplay, insane movement mechanics, and constant updates, and you’ve got a game that rewards players who want to grind and compete. If you’ve got a solid squad and you’re ready to sweat a little, Apex Legends isn’t just a good game it’s built for teams that want to dominate. High skill ceiling. High intensity. Pure teamwork. If you want competitive? This is it.

review:
We all remember the tweet. “Titanfall is the very core of our DNA. Who knows what the future holds...”

That was years ago. Since then, the "DNA" has felt more like a marketing gimmick than a development philosophy. We’ve watched Apex grow into a billion-dollar machine while Titanfall 2 was left to fight for its life against server attacks and neglect.

Now, we are six months out from the 10th Anniversary. 

We’ve seen the focus shift to Highguard and the endless cycle of Apex collection events, but the community that built Respawn’s reputation is still here, and we’re tired. We don’t want another "Northstar" charm in a $20 bundle. We want you to respect the franchise that made you.

If October 28th passes and all we get is a "Happy Birthday" tweet and a sale on Apex Coins, then the message is clear: the DNA is dead. You have six months to show us that Titanfall actually means something to you. Don't let the 10-year mark be the day we finally stop caring.

review:
After spending a lot of hours in this game, I can confidently say it’s one of the most polished and mechanically satisfying battle royales out there. It’s not just about aim — it’s about movement, positioning, and smart ability usage. When everything clicks with your squad, it’s one of the most adrenaline-pumping FPS experiences you can get.

✅ What I Like:

Top-tier movement – Sliding, wall bouncing, zipline plays… movement actually feels rewarding and expressive.

Smooth gunplay – Weapons feel distinct, balanced, and satisfying to use.

Legend abilities matter – Each Legend brings real value to the team; comp and synergy actually make a difference.

Ping system – Still one of the best non-verbal communication systems in any multiplayer game.

High skill ceiling – You can always improve mechanically and strategically.

❌ What Can Be Frustrating:

Ranked can get very sweaty – Not always chill-friendly.

Matchmaking can feel inconsistent – Sometimes you get cracked opponents back-to-back.

Random teammates – Either the best synergy ever… or pure chaos.

💭 Overall:

If you enjoy competitive shooters that reward smart aggression, teamwork, and clean mechanics, this game delivers. It can be intense and occasionally tilting, but the clutch moments and high-speed fights make it worth it. Still one of the best FPS games to grind. 🔥

review:
The game mechanics are fun and the gunplay is very arcadish in style so it can give you some fun and the game in its core is fun to play. 
The reason to avoid it is the ranked gameplay is more of a Pubs + experience with hot dropping in one spot with 10+ teams is seen as top entertainment. Random queue is pure torture where the majority of your gameplay will be like you dying without a weapon or overrun by 3-4 teams attacking you from each side just seconds after landing.
The tried to fix that by adding designated landing zones and for a time the game was playable again but for some odd reason it was removed but added back but only for the higher ranked lobbies. So if you enjoy a landing zone simulator then this is your game. 
If you are looking for a tactical BR where positioning and tactical choices are rewarded then move along, this is chaotic torture for any casual gamer. If they remove the dropships and go back to designated dropzones i might return but i for now am out, since this is not a fun game at all.

review:
I played this game very early on before I new of Titan Fall I then grew to love TF2, but I still came back to apex I made some amazing friends playing apex it was fun the story being built felt amazing and left me wanting more especially hearing of the posibility of an animated series so many years ago. During season 3 Meltdown I felt like we were getting something so much bigger with cryptos lore then came loba and revenant which I loved too, but slowly more characters were added more lore the story that was building felt like it just stopped which I felt saddend by.
Slowly more stuff was added things were buffed nerfed taken away sometimes because of streamers complaining the game started to feel off the constant changing in things felt like the game became something else which it has, don't get me wrong I love that the devs are passionate and can only do so much at the whim of greedy ea devs you guys are awesome. But it slowly lost what made me love it so many items just feel out of place and forced lore and and possible stories scrapped just to continue the game.
Bangalore for example her brother was supposudly dead her whole reason of joining the apex games to get money to leave became meaningless then she had her brother back. crypto's sister was taken his goal to find her then she supposudly escaped and was in hiding but then the story just ended there no confirmation just ended, yet years later crypto is still just fighting in the games after going to different planets why? it feels like he just became another hollow character, these characters have motives but they get cut to continue the game. Season 4 is when it really just ended for me because we had so much build only to have nothing. The game would've been better off staying in the same gameplay state of season 3 or 4  No constant damage changes or removing items just the game as it is with lore and new maps and cosmetics that's it. Now if I jump into a match I everything feels out of place and makes no sense, elements of a sandbox but no sandbox possibilites. When I jump in to Titanfall 2 or other shooters I can tell exactly what I'm looking at I can say Oh! yep this is Titanfall or this is CSGO or heck this Is Cyberpunk, Darksouls you name it, Heck looking at old Apex season 1 and 2 I look and if I played It I would know and feel and understand oh this Is Apex, but that gone. Yes things will change or even never exist again which is normal but the Apex I loved is gone.
Devs I don't blame you's, It's not your fault, It's EA's so keep being passionate devs and for anyone who read through my horrible cluster ♥♥♥♥ of a random time at 4AM where I felt like reminiscing on the past thank you be safe and you're welcome for the brain cells you lost XD got your brain cells and you'll never get them back XD.

review:
One of the better takes on the battle royale genre.

When it comes to things I like about the game, it has a ton of legends to pick from, and everything is usable, nothing sticks out as overly broken or underpowered, though obviously some things are better (or worse) than others. It also runs pretty well given how good it looks. Progression is solid: the first form is your player level and unlocking all the legends. With a ton of game modes available, leveling up and getting all the legends feels rewarding but won't take ages. Most legends take a lot of time to learn and master to their fullest potential, even the simpler ones. The second form of progression is pushing your rank, it's generally easy to climb to gold if you just play, but after that, the difficulty ramps up and the real grind begins. 

When it comes to things I don't like, it's generally how greedy/expensive it feels, a lot of cool skins cost more than I'd like, but you're not forced to buy them, so this is a minor complaint. A much bigger one is how teammates piss me off every time I solo queue: they WILL insta-drop, then yell at you for not helping when they try to walk down the other 12 squads because they think they're nice like that while playing Octane or Wraith.

review:
Overall I have to give Apex a thumbs down in its current state despite having previously invested a lot of time (and money) into the game.  As an aside, I can see why publishers chase after the next big live services game...I never realized it but Steam has a little section that shows you how much you spent in-game and my jaw dropped :)  I think I'll avoid F2P for now.

I really liked it during it's first 3 years which I considered "peak" Apex when it exhibited a lot of charm and the soundtrack at the time was so "chef's kiss" but it's kind of devolved into an absolute CACOPHONY and clutter.  Some players might like adapting to an ever changing meta or having to keep up with the different items they've added (and removed) over time, but for the average player, it's just way too much.

But there are pros.  Big pros.  Gunplay being the #1 pro.  Since day 1, the gunplay still feels fantastic.  Sometimes I just like going into the firing range and just shoot the guns.  Each gun is unique and takes skill to master and know their ins and outs.  Overall the balancing has improved over time but certainly early on there were OP weapons that players liked to flock to.

2nd biggest pro is the movement.  It's fast and fluid and stays true to Titanfall roots.  There are movement legends like Octane/Wraith that are fun to play as and minimizes the potential for "boredom" or "lulls" in battle royale.  There's a lot of movement skill you can achieve in this game alone and top streamers have certainly showcased how the sky's the limit.  Very rarely do I find games where I actually don't mind just running around in a running simulator, they did a good job here.

Now as for cons, this game is not fun at all playing solo.  Both pubs and ranked are sweaty as hell.  Wildcard mode is a bit better and the mixtape (collection of TDM, gun game, control) is fine but gets old over time  There's some algo that puts you in "easier" lobbies if you haven't played in a long time but once you do "well" so to speak, you're put into the sweatiest lobbies known to mankind playing against those that live, breathe, and poop Apex 24/7.  In other words, this is not a game to decompress to after work.  This is not the game for "chill Saturday."  In fact, your toxicity level and blood pressure will rise greatly the more you play.  I used to habitually crack open a beer while playing this game because it kept me from treating this game so damn seriously.  If there was prox chat, I'd be banned in no time.  Well, one can say "just git gud man, you'll enjoy the competitiveness of it" - but I actually enjoyed "gitting gud" in other games, just not this one.  

On the other hand, it can be a fun game to play with friends or those who play as a team and communicate.  I've always cherished those moments.

review:
Apex Legends is a very competitive game that I believe hard to safely recommend. At its core what we have here is a high skill gap shooter complete with layers of complexity such as movement tech, character synergies and gun recoil management.

I think almost every competitive game on the market can be enjoyed in a more casual setting through unranked game modes but it strangely does not seem the case with Apex Legends.

There are systems in place to cater towards a more casual experience like a bot battle royale game mode and special rotating game modes that can be more approachable but I choose to address the main game mode here.

Nearly every unranked match I ended up either paired with or paired against people that have obtained the master rank and sometimes even the predator rank which is rather unbalanced, I frankly do not understand matchmaking and how it all works out but it is tiresome to see you immediately get thrown in the same lobbies as people that could already be playing on a semi professional level.

Now being a team based game I believe it is definitely designed to be played with a pre existing group of people even if there is a really good non verbal communication system in place.

Monetization is ultimately what has disappointed me the most, Apex Legends makes money through enforcing FOMO as well as heavily promoting gambling, it saddens me how viable such predatory tactics are and I simply wish things were different.

So for me it will be a non recommendation even if I will remember certain characters of the game fondly for their personalities ❤

review:
Maintaining the Predator and Master title in Apex Legends and more is the perfect illusion to bamboozle you one more time, just so EA can stick it to you in your face like a sadistic barista who purposely screws up your order, then spits in it, blends it up, and serves it to you while hollering your name wrong just to let you know that they literally, figuratively, metaphorically, and mentally own you. It’s less of a flex and more of a curse because the moment you hit that Master status, your life does a 180 instaswap dankdank skibidi toilet rizz 360noscope and instantly becomes 5000% more depressing. You’ll stare blankly at your screen, reminiscing about the sleepless nights you spent grinding for this title, and you’ve unlocked an endless void of self-inflicted suffering, where betrayal is handed out like free samples from Costco. You’ll find yourself wondering if your teamates are secretly auditioning for a role in the "From Apex Predator to Apex Prey" documentary, because at this point, their incompetence feels like performance art and their brains must be studied or donated because clearly they have never been used. Every game becomes a unique experience where you’re left questioning your life choices while flexing an heirloom that costs more than your car insurance, just to have something pretty to look at while you're tucked into a corner like a disciplined Victorian child, ratting into the top 4 spot with -8rp. You’ll be mid-fight when the game decides to enter a sub-atomic frame-rate aneurysm, forcing you to watch your own digital execution in agonizing, underwater ultra-delay (it’s not a bug), it’s a cinematic funeral for your K/D designed to let you image count every single bullet while the server hamster has a stroke in real-time. While the core mechanics rot, Respawn decides the meta really needed literal windows added to the maps because clearly, we needed a better view of our own misery... and while Bloodhound scans into the abyss like a lost puppy just to confirm that yes, God has indeed abandoned us. You’ll be standing there in a silent building when a full three-stack of sweats lands directly on your neck with zero audio, because apparently, footsteps are a premium legacy feature that we simply cannot afford. EA isn’t just taking your time, they’re slurping up your soul, one battle pass at a time, 1 dollar at a time, 1 second at a time, and by clicking "Ready," you agree to donate your central nervous system to the EA salt mines, acknowledging that your low sodium teammates are programmed to ignore your pings and land solo as wraith, while you waive your right to sanity. Even Satan won’t be able to help you cope, he already threw in the towel at season 7 because he was a hard-stuck Gold 4 in a world where EA was the Predator. You aren't getting an heirloom either unless you play this game for decades or pay $4k in undeductible credit because you no longer are playing the game, you are the game that's getting played. Pay the tax and pay your dues and for the love of everything unholy, stop simping over Loba because her tactical hasn't worked since the Obama administration, but you’ll still buy her next $160 skin because your thirst is the only thing more infinite than EA’s server lag.

Teammates??? Dont exist. Respawn??? Useless. Balance the game??? Flip a coin. Server hamsters??? Deceased. Recommend this game??? Yes. Hotel??? Trivago.

review:
[quote][b]Warning: Third Party Account[/b]

This game requires you to create a third party "EA" account... there's no valid reason for this in a PC game when SteamWorks is available.  This is often done to harvest private data and profile you, with potential sale and transmission of your private data to third parties.  The developers will probably argue that you can trust them, but if they were trustworthy they wouldn't demand you create a third party account.

This represents a considerable risk to gamers due to the privacy and security implications of developers that wish to bypass Steam and harvest user data.  Remember, sometimes if the product is free, YOU are the product.[/quote]

[quote][b][url=https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microtransaction]Warning: In-Game Cash Shop[/url][/b]

This game is infested with a toxic mobile app style cash shop.  You will be prompted to and expected to give money/credit card details to the developers through this cash shop to buy in-game goods so you can play the game.[/quote]

It's always a bit tough to review games that are (were) highly popular.

"Apex Legends™" is the big AAA ripoff of Fortnite, itself a ripoff of PUBG.  Whew.  Big money corporations sure get angry when their IP is stolen, but they have no qualms about stealing other people's games entirely.  For profit. Weird how that works.

Anyway EA were keen to get some of that PUBG/Fortnite money so they made this "me too" for 12 year olds.  It's a sci-fi extraction shooter riddled with battle passes and cosmetic MTX through the roof... you can't move an inch without being slapped in the face with a demand for your money.  Don't see this as free for a minute.

Almost all of the core mechanics for Apex Legends were unabashedly stolen from PUBG.  This is a PvP extraction shooter.  You drop with your team from the sky, with barely any gear.  You run around (a lot, most of the gameplay time is this) looting buildings full of randomly distributed guns and gear.  If you encounter other players you shoot them or you get shot.  If you are shot, you are "knocked" and you need a teammate to ressurrect you.  If they don't, you're out of the game and can't play until the next round.

To ensure this gets more "interesting", EA also stole the "restricting play area" mechanic from PUBG, too.  In fact it's hard to describe this as anything but a reskin of PUBG.  Talk about corporate creativity.

Last team standing wins.  That applies to both the players in the game and the coporations gleefully stealing each others Battle Royale game design.

This suffers from all the same problems that other PvP heavy games do, only 10 times worse.  It's rife with cheaters.  EA does a really bad job of managing this.  It's rife with player toxicity.  EA does a really bad job of managing this.  Other players tend to take one look at their teammates and they'll just leave instead of sticking around and trying to help, so randoms will invariably screw you over.

The graphics in this game are really quite good, which at least makes the game look more appealing.  And while it's vital for any game to have impressive visuals (gaming is a visual medium, after all), there's more to a game than just the graphics.  To be worth a gamers time, the game must match top tier visuals with top tier gameplay, and that's not happening here, for those reasons I mentioned.

Really, in terms of technical execution, it's got everything you would ask for in a triple-A franchise delivery and that's applaudable... the real heroes of game development, the professional artists and programmers getting their paychecks from EA did an exceptional job.  But there's no single player or narrative content here, it's soulless compared to a lot of other shooters, and the whole thing is heavily designed around profit.  Here, we draw a distinction between the awesome, professional, highly skilled programmers and artists that are absolutely necessary for any game to be successful... and the accountants who replaced game designers and make all the big decisions. 

The upshot of all of that is that Apex Legends is the palest imitation of the competition and the community problems and toxic, gamer hostile monetisation has lead to this to start bleeding off the player base.  And this is bleeding heavily.  The game peaked at an insanely good population, well over 600,000 players.  Now... they're struggling to exceed 60,000.  Dropping player population down to 10% of peak... and this continues to trend downwards... means this game will be completely dead in, at best, a year or so.  

For comparison, PUBG typically runs 400k+ players at any given time... most days they're around the all-time peak Apex Legends ever achieved.  I'm not saying you should go play PUBG, but just to demonstrate that Apex Legends is not the winning horse in this race.

So if you're looking to get into this now... I think you may have missed the boat.

You're forced to sign a highly questionable yet legally unenforceable End User License Agreement... the big corps love these, but they are toxic and hostile to gamers.  Make no mistake, that EULA exists to attempt to harm and control you, for the benefit of EA.  This agreement includes questionable requirements that may attempt to revoke consumer law protections that gamers expect to have, which is also highly problematic.  Developers must learn they are here to serve gamers, not control them.  I had my cat walk over the keyboard while the EULA was displaying, which fixed that problem for me.

Now on to pricing and value for gamers.

This game is infested with mobile app store like microtransactions.  This is a consumer hostile model that we don't need to see on PC.  It's deceptively priced to gouge gamers, and that's a reason in itself to avoid this.

Given that this free game won't increase your game collector count, is it worth your time to add it to your library?

This is a "maybe" experience... if you have a lot of free time on your hands and nothing else to do, why not?  But for the purposes of a review, this isn't something I can say everyone must try.  It's not quite there.  Valve doesn't give us a "maybe" option in reviews, but if they did, that's what I'd choose for this.

review:
I am a new PC Gamer so my logged time on steam isn't an accurate representation of the actual experience I've had with this game.  I've played since season one, however I didn't really get into the game until Season 4 when they introduced revenant.  I am a revenant main with over 8,000 kills with him.  That being said, I'm an old man with a family and multiple jobs and responsibilities.  I am a casual gamer and usually play overnights.

Apex is a fun game.  My best friend has played this game since it has came out and he is the apex team worst nightmare.  That's because he hasn't spent a dime on the game.  The game has evolved into a micro transactions superstore similar to fortnite.  I understand the game has to make a profit, but they've also reduced the grinding rewards.  I am sitting with like, 400,000 apex tokens that are essentially worthless.  These can only be used to recolor a skin after you already spent actually money.  Now you can upgrade an apex pack and increase the likelihood of getting something from your wishlist, but they are still next to useless.

Gameplay is fine.  It's responsive and although there are some cheaters, it's far better than a Warzone.  I'll play warzone and leave any given gaming session knowing I was murdered by a hacker.  That feeling is far less prominent in Apex.  Don't get me wrong, it's still there, but far less frequent.

If you have children, be advised there is some political messaging in this game.  Many of the characters are gay and non-binary.  Some are trans and some of the comics and artwork from the lore depict gay kissing etc.  During the Black Lives Matter riots, they had a pop up alert supporting the cause that everyone could see.  They had trans alerts pop up, suicidal ideation alerts, etc.  The game is T for teen.  They also featured a lot of foreign nations flags, but you cannot use the United States flag as it's simply not there.  BLM, ALM, etc. could be used as a clan tag, but TRUMP or that ilk were not allowed.

The developers listen to the community, sometimes, and they do a decent job of buffing and nerfing particular legends.  They do release a lot of the same skins and use different coloring and say hey, pay us money, but it's whatever.  

In summation, it's a decent game and it works as intended.  They'll nerf a legend to where it's downright unplayable, then buff it in a later season with additional cosmetics for sale.  They do this routinely. They do the same with guns so it'll force everyone to use the same loadout because you'll be at a disadvantage if you don't. For instance right now, the shotguns are buffed again so everyone is using them.   When you can do 100 plus damage from a shotgun in one trigger pull, why would you use anything else?

review:
Haven't played in a hot minute, but Apex, despite plenty of flaws and controversies, does at least succeed in having a solid set of foundations to provide a rewarding play experience from. The gunplay is excellent, with few if any weapons feeling truly unviable, provided you're willing to get familiar with them. Combat speed is also addicting, with the pace of firefights and movetech offering a fluid and dynamic rush. And while more limited in scope and availability than its main battle royale mode, a number of alternate gamemodes it's rotated through over the years do a decent job at providing opportunities for focus on particular areas of the game outside of the standard loot n shoot formula.

For a ftp fps title, it's worth at least trying out. Of course you're balancing all of these positives against the usual issues you'll be used to if you've ever played something published by EA before, so the decision ultimately lies with you.

review: I've been playing video games since as early as I can remember, and at the ripe old age of 40 years old, I have played a lot of video games. My earliest memories include trying to find various ways of getting Mario offscreen in Super Mario 2... And I love this walking simulator.  Maybe I just have a video game addiction... but having tasted many of the poisons this world offers, this one is very fine.  On par with that of the puffer fish, a true delicacy.  It's not for every body, but that's why roblox and minecraft exists.  You can roll all the loot RNG you want without any of the PVP in those games.  The skill ceiling is incredibly high with higher levels of play being incredibly unforgiving... but good matches and wins can be had.  By design of the game, only 3 out of every 60 players will be winners.  That's 95% of all players will be losers, and that fact can be incredibly frustrating (it makes me so toxic sometimes).  Still, if all you ate in life was chocolate ice cream, your life would still be bland, regardless of how decadent.  Life is about contrast and overcoming obstacles, and this game is all about contrast and grinding obstacles that need to be overcome.  Honestly, I love how OP everything has become, it is a lot to learn and keep up to date with... but no longer do legends feel like soldier classes, and more like interstellar mercenaries.  From those early noob days to what the game currently is, its so much more intense.  You can just feel how the developers labored over every aspect of the game with a fine tooth comb, from the animations to the game mechanics, a lot of thought and effort has been put into this game, and it shows.  Its not the best game, but its the best game that's currently out.  Sure, there are issues, but I think a lot of that is just because the game is a for profit venture.  And sure, the great masters were paid for their work, but that was mostly because they couldn't afford the materials themselves.  Many worked in-between their own desire of experimentation and the desires of their patrons.

review:
People love to call Apex Legends a fast, exciting hero shooter. But talk to frustrated fans, and it starts to sound more like a lesson in wasted potential. When Apex first showed up, it really shook up the genre. That excitement faded. Now, a lot of players say the game feels like a slog, and you spend more time wrestling with shady monetization and bugs than actually fighting in the arena. The core gameplay? Yeah, it’s still tight. But everything else just leaves a sour aftertaste.

The biggest complaint is the way the game chases your wallet. Cosmetics cost a ridiculous amount. Limited-time events are just pricey bundles in disguise. And those heirlooms everyone wants? Good luck—unless you’re ready to grind forever or pay up, they’re out of reach. People who’ve already sunk hours and money into Apex get bombarded with more reasons to spend. Sure, free games need to make cash, but here, the balance is way off. It doesn’t feel fair. It feels like they’re wringing players for every cent.

Then there’s all the psychological tricks. Limited-time skins, battle passes, ranked resets, rotating shops—it’s all designed to keep you hooked and worried you’ll miss out. After a while, you start to wonder: are you still having fun, or just logging in so you don’t fall behind? For a lot of people, that’s where it crosses the line. It’s not engaging anymore—it’s manipulative.

Respawn, the studio behind Apex, catches a lot of heat for this. Players complain about server crashes, audio bugs, weird matchmaking, and balance problems that never seem to go away. A new patch drops and, somehow, old problems stick around and new ones pop up. Critics call out Respawn for focusing on new skins and events instead of actually fixing the game.

Communication doesn’t help. Some players feel like devs only speak up when it benefits them. The same complaints show up every season—about ranked, cheaters, broken characters—and nothing really changes. Even when the devs do talk, it rarely leads anywhere.

Don’t forget account security and bans. Plenty of players say they’ve lost accounts to hackers or got banned for no clear reason. Appeals are slow, confusing, and feel like you’re talking to a robot. People want fairness, but when nobody explains what happened, it just makes them angrier.

And then there’s EA—the publisher with the worst reputation in gaming. Most players blame them for the high prices and aggressive event structures. The feeling is that the people in charge care more about squeezing out profits than making something great. That builds a ton of distrust.

Maybe the worst part? Loyalty feels like it means nothing. Players who’ve stuck around for years don’t get better servers or less cheating—they get more overpriced skins. That just proves money matters more than the actual game.

So, it really comes down to what you want. The movement and gunplay? Still some of the best. But for a lot of critics, all that good stuff gets buried under greedy monetization, bad communication, and account messes. Apex could be incredible. Instead, it keeps throwing away that potential for quick profits and engagement stats, while trust and real satisfaction fall by the wayside.

The gameplay can be fun, but recent changes to ranked make soloQ awful, but the main thing:

*deplorable* monetisation. I am someone who is happy to pay for a seasonal battle pass in an F2P game, but Apex has to have the lowest value pass in any game i've played. They have split the battle pass, and added different paid for tiers, and all you get is a couple of skins. The top skins (heirlooms) are not part of passes and cost upwards of 200 bucks, which is insanity. They are techincally from "events" but the events are just lootboxes you buy.

The free currency you earn cant really be used at all for decent skins (like you could make it exceptionally high cost, but NOTHING good to use the free currency for is crazy business). The free pack rate is slow enough that its hard to accumulate the actually valuable currencies that way.

The greed always leaves a bad taste.

review:
This game is as toxic as all of my past relationships, but I'm a masochist so I keep coming back!

To anyone with sense, do not install this game in its current state, the new season has bricked the game for both casual and competitive play. Server issues are worse, high ping in trios, duos effectively swept under the rug, rubberbanding in ranked. Hit reg? Never heard of it.

The most annoying part. The gunplay, the movement, the legends, the abilities and the way they can all tie together in a competitive game playing with your homies is absolutely unbeaten by any other game I've played. Unfortunately you almost never get to enjoy it because as soon as you do find a good fight the server crashes or a pred stack appears out of nowhere to Rhaegal your whole team (she just kind of forgot).  But damn brothers, the moments when you've dominated the early game, rotated perfectly, beaten a third, fourth, fifth and sixth party to claim a win as the final circle closes. WOOF WOOF.

I love this game and I'll play for as long as its kept alive because I have no self respect, I want to believe every time they say "hey legends, we heard you and we're going to fix..." that they will. I'll crawl back every time. Another layer of white makeup applied, a new shiny red nose. 

Do yourself a favour and don't bother.

review:
Apex Legends to jedna z tych gier battle royale, którym udało się nie tylko przebić do świadomości graczy, ale też utrzymać silną pozycję przez długi czas dzięki konsekwentnie rozwijanej formule. Produkcja Respawn Entertainment wyróżnia się przede wszystkim wyjątkowo płynną mechaniką ruchu, dopracowanym strzelaniem oraz mocnym naciskiem na współpracę w trzyosobowych drużynach.

Już od pierwszych minut czuć, że fundamenty rozgrywki zostały zaprojektowane z dużą precyzją. Poruszanie się po mapie — ślizgi, wspinaczka, szybkie zmiany pozycji — daje ogromną swobodę i sprawia, że starcia są dynamiczne, ale jednocześnie czytelne. System broni jest intuicyjny, a różnice między poszczególnymi typami uzbrojenia mają realny wpływ na styl walki, co zachęca do eksperymentowania.

Największą tożsamością Apex Legends są jednak legendy, czyli grywalne postacie z unikalnymi umiejętnościami. Zdolności taktyczne i ostateczne wprowadzają warstwę strategiczną wykraczającą poza samo celowanie, a odpowiednie łączenie umiejętności w drużynie potrafi przesądzić o wyniku meczu. Dzięki temu gra nagradza nie tylko refleks, lecz także komunikację i planowanie.

Na uznanie zasługuje również tempo rozwoju tytułu. Regularne sezony wprowadzają nowe postacie, zmiany na mapach i świeżą zawartość kosmetyczną, co pomaga utrzymać zainteresowanie społeczności. Jednocześnie rdzeń rozgrywki pozostaje spójny, dzięki czemu Apex nie traci swojej tożsamości mimo licznych aktualizacji.

Apex Legends najlepiej trafia do graczy, którzy szukają połączenia dynamicznej akcji z drużynową strategią. To battle royale bardziej techniczne i wymagające współpracy niż wiele konkurencyjnych produkcji, ale właśnie dzięki temu zwycięstwo daje tu wyjątkową satysfakcję.

review:
I returned to the game after a while (over 1k hour on origin since S3). All in all I do recommend it, you can play it fast and loose: drop hot and fight for solid 5 minutes, or careful and calculating and loot without firing a single shot for 10. Its a great game, which can be enjoyed solo and premade, but there are some glaring issues I still see. some new, some for more than 6 years (out of which I played for 2-3):

Cons:
Matchmaking in pubs and ranked is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, how can my barely above 1.0KD ass go against masters and preds every single game. Not 1 or 2, but over half of them. My dude, the closest to owning 20kill badge was in my dream, and in my games I see it on every other person. My random is lvl 20 and still doesnt know all legend abilities and we face top200 pred and I have yo yell at him how Alter ult works.
Ranked is inconsistent, one season drop ship, one season POI drop. One season we value placement and people hide, another we want kills. Lets pick a direction. Lets rewards trick shots, clutches and other cool ♥♥♥♥ on lower ranks and lets focus on actual placement in higher scene.
Queueing: lets make solo only Q please. Ranked, pubs, both. Doesnt matter. I enjoy this game but getting wiped but 3 premade sweats every other game isnt my description of a good game.
UI: I returned after more than a year and it took me solid 20 minutes to get my bearing whats what. As UI in-game is fairly clean, UI in main menu is trash and just too much, take store tab: atm there are 9 different 'levels' u can scroll to, of which 5 can be scrolled side to side. And then there are 3 other tabs 2 of which are equally confusing (special offers? and mythics).
Aint gonna complain about cost of mythics or rarity of heirloom shard, thats an old news right. But I gotta mention 7th anniversary event. Oh when oh when for anniversary we got 50% off for the items and packs. Now? instead of 24 items there's 36 effectively increasing the price by 50% to finish the event (yeah yeah there are 2 red rewards instead of one, but doesnt feel very celebratory to me). So instead of 70$ to get the guaranteed and coveted heirloom shards, we pay 260$. Yay, lets celebrate!

review:
絶対にこのゲームをインストールしちゃだめ！！！

Apex Legendsって、「神ゲーのフリをしたストレス生成装置」だと思ってる。いや本当に。褒められてる部分は確かにある。でもそれ全部、「ちゃんと機能してれば」の話なんだよね。

まず前提として、このゲームは気持ちよく勝つ体を極端にケチる設計になってる。バトロワだから？違う。問題はそこじゃない。勝てない理由が自分の実力に帰結しない場面が多すぎる**こと。マッチングは平気で崩壊してるし、内部レートはブラックボックス、初心者・中級者・プレデターが同じ卓に放り込まれることすら珍しくない。で、何が起きるかって？当然、努力とか関係なく轢き殺されるだけ。

「いやそれでも上手くなればいいじゃん」っていう人いるけど、その“上手くなる過程”をゲーム側が全力で邪魔してくるのがこのタイトルの終わってるところ。ラグ、パケロス、謎のヒット判定。撃ち勝ったと思ったらデスログだけ流れるあの虚無感、もはや様式美。FPSとして一番大事な“撃ったら当たる、当たったら倒せる”っていう最低限の信頼関係すら揺らいでる。

で、運営。ここが一番冷笑ポイント高い。調整の遅さはまだいいとして、ズレ方が芸術的。明らかに壊れてる要素は数ヶ月放置、でもなぜか“そこ？”って部分をピンポイントでナーフ。結果、環境は一向に健全化せず、「結局この武器とこのキャラでいいよね」っていう思考停止メタが完成する。選択肢が多そうに見えて、実際は狭い。

チーター問題も、もはや語るのが面倒なくらい常連。特に上位帯は「うまい人」と「不正してる人」の区別がつかないレベルで混ざる。で、真面目にやってる側が疑心暗鬼になる。これ、対戦ゲームとしてかなり致命的なんだけど、慣れすぎて“そういうもの”扱いされてるのがまた終わってる。

あと、個人的に一番しんどいのが精神的な消耗効率の悪さ。1マッチにかかる時間、漁り、移動、接敵、そして即死。これを繰り返して得られるものが「-RP」とか普通にある。しかも野良だと意思疎通はほぼ運ゲー。味方が強ければ神ゲー、弱ければ地獄。この振れ幅を“スリル”って呼ぶのは無理がある。ただのストレスガチャ。

それでもこのゲームが一定の人気を維持してる理由？単純に噛み合った瞬間の快感が異常に強いから。それだけ。逆に言えば、それ以外の時間はほぼ全部その一瞬のための苦行。冷静に考えるとコスパ最悪の娯楽だと思う。

総評すると、Respawn Entertainmentの技術力とセンスで作られた土台の上に、運営の迷走と放置が積み重なってできた惜しいを通り越して疲れるゲーム。
ハマってる人は否定しないけど、「これから始める？」って聞かれたら、笑って「やめとけ」って言う。

——まあ、それでもまた起動してしまうあたり、こっちも大概なんだけどね。

review:
Apex Legends is the worst game ever made

Apex Legends can also feel like the worst game ever made when you boot it up hoping for a fun session and instead get thrown into a psychological endurance test disguised as matchmaking. Somehow, no matter your skill level, the game has an uncanny ability to place you against fully coordinated three-stacks with thousands of kills, perfect aim, and zero mercy. Casual matches feel sweatier than ranked, ranked feels like punishment, and every loss feels less like a lesson and more like a personal attack. The emotional whiplash of getting deleted before you even find a gun can make the game feel cruel rather than competitive.

Then there are the technical issues, which have haunted Apex like a curse since day one. Audio cuts out at the worst possible moments, footsteps vanish into the void, and enemies appear behind you as if they teleported straight from another dimension. Servers lag, rubber-band, or crash entirely, turning high-stakes fights into coin flips decided by latency instead of skill. Nothing drains joy faster than losing a hard-earned fight not because you played poorly, but because the game itself simply decided not to function.

Balance changes often add fuel to the fire, swinging wildly from overpowered chaos to underwhelming irrelevance with little warning. A legend you spent seasons mastering can suddenly become useless overnight, while another dominates the meta unchecked for months. Weapons get nerfed into obscurity or buffed into nightmares, making the game feel inconsistent and frustrating to keep up with. Instead of rewarding dedication, the shifting meta can make players feel punished for investing time into anything that isn’t currently favored.

Worst of all is the emotional trap Apex creates, where frustration, addiction, and hope blend into one exhausting loop. You get angry, swear you’re uninstalling, complain about everything that’s wrong with the game—and then immediately queue up again because no other shooter quite scratches the same itch. Apex Legends becomes the worst game ever made not because it’s bad, but because it’s almost perfect, which makes every flaw hurt ten times more. It’s the kind of game you love deeply, hate loudly, and can never fully escape.

review: Recommend, but barely. I like it a lot. I played this more on other systems, which explains the low playtime here. The movement feels fast and clean. The arena shooter setup works well, with large maps that still support smart positioning and rotations. Legends have clear roles built around abilities rather than gear. Balance issues are noticeable, and solo queue is difficult. Team coordination matters more than raw aim. There’s light lore in the background, but it’s mostly an excuse for the matches. I haven’t played in a while, but I still think about coming back. Probably only if I can rope friends into it.
